source_paper_title: The Competition Complexity of Dynamic Pricing
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Brustle_2024, title={The Competition Complexity of Dynamic Pricing}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0230}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0230}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Brustle, Johannes and Correa, José and Duetting, Paul and Verdugo, Victor}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1986–2008} }
context:
Page 9, Section 2.5 (end of Theorem 4 discussion)

Our lower bound for the Competition Complexity in Theorem 4 uses \(m − 1\) MHR distributions and one regular distribution. It remains open whether the Competition Complexity is sublinear for the case of \(m\) independent and MHR stages.
title: Determine sublinear competition complexity for independent monotone hazard rate stages
background_latex:
Consider a dynamic auction with 
1) a single seller, 
2) a fixed set of buyers present in every stage, and 
3) one item sold in each of \(m\) consecutive stages.

In each stage \(k\in\{1,\dots,m\}\), each buyer \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) draws a private value \(v_i^k\) for that stage's item. Values are independent across buyers. Stages are independent in the sense that, for each buyer, the random variables \(v_i^1,\dots,v_i^m\) are independent across \(k\). The (common, i.i.d. across buyers) marginal distribution at stage \(k\) is a continuous distribution \(D_k\) on \(\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) with cumulative distribution function \(F_k\) and density \(f_k\).

A continuous distribution \(D\) with cdf \(F\) and density \(f\) is monotone hazard rate (MHR) if its hazard rate
\[
 h(x)=\frac{f(x)}{1-F(x)}
\]
is nondecreasing in \(x\) on its support.

Let \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) denote the optimal expected revenue achievable by a revenue-maximizing incentive-compatible dynamic mechanism with \(n\) buyers over \(m\) stages when the full stochastic environment is \(X\) (here, independent stages with marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\)). Let \(\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\) denote the expected revenue of running a second-price auction (VCG for a single item) separately in each stage with \(n+c\) buyers, where the extra \(c\) buyers are also i.i.d. from the same stage distributions and participate in every stage.

The competition complexity (with respect to stagewise VCG) is the minimum \(c\ge 0\) such that
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
(Equivalently, the smallest number of additional buyers needed so that simple stagewise second-price auctions match the optimal dynamic auction's expected revenue.)
problem_definition_latex:
In the setting above with independent stages and continuous MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), determine the asymptotic growth (in \(n\)) of the competition complexity \(c\) needed for stagewise VCG to achieve
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
In particular, decide whether there exists a function \(c(n)=o(n)\) (possibly depending on \(m\) but not on the specific MHR distributions) such that for every choice of continuous MHR stage distributions \(D_1,\dots,D_m\) with independent stages,
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c(n),m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
latex:
Consider a dynamic auction with 
1) a single seller, 
2) a fixed set of buyers present in every stage, and 
3) one item sold in each of \(m\) consecutive stages.

In each stage \(k\in\{1,\dots,m\}\), each buyer \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) draws a private value \(v_i^k\) for that stage's item. Values are independent across buyers. Stages are independent in the sense that, for each buyer, the random variables \(v_i^1,\dots,v_i^m\) are independent across \(k\). The (common, i.i.d. across buyers) marginal distribution at stage \(k\) is a continuous distribution \(D_k\) on \(\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) with cumulative distribution function \(F_k\) and density \(f_k\).

A continuous distribution \(D\) with cdf \(F\) and density \(f\) is monotone hazard rate (MHR) if its hazard rate
\[
 h(x)=\frac{f(x)}{1-F(x)}
\]
is nondecreasing in \(x\) on its support.

Let \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) denote the optimal expected revenue achievable by a revenue-maximizing incentive-compatible dynamic mechanism with \(n\) buyers over \(m\) stages when the full stochastic environment is \(X\) (here, independent stages with marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\)). Let \(\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\) denote the expected revenue of running a second-price auction (VCG for a single item) separately in each stage with \(n+c\) buyers, where the extra \(c\) buyers are also i.i.d. from the same stage distributions and participate in every stage.

The competition complexity (with respect to stagewise VCG) is the minimum \(c\ge 0\) such that
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
(Equivalently, the smallest number of additional buyers needed so that simple stagewise second-price auctions match the optimal dynamic auction's expected revenue.)

In the setting above with independent stages and continuous MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), determine the asymptotic growth (in \(n\)) of the competition complexity \(c\) needed for stagewise VCG to achieve
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
In particular, decide whether there exists a function \(c(n)=o(n)\) (possibly depending on \(m\) but not on the specific MHR distributions) such that for every choice of continuous MHR stage distributions \(D_1,\dots,D_m\) with independent stages,
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c(n),m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
open_problem_quote: Our lower bound for the Competition Complexity in Theorem 4 uses \(m − 1\) MHR distributions and one regular distribution. It remains open whether the Competition Complexity is sublinear for the case of \(m\) independent and MHR stages.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search over parametric families of continuous MHR distributions (e.g., piecewise-exponential hazards) and simulate VCG and candidate upper bounds on \(\mathrm{OPT}\) to look for instances that force \(c\) to be linear. However, exactly computing \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) for dynamic mechanisms is generally hard, so the search would rely on relaxations/upper bounds, limiting decisiveness.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary loop could represent stage distributions via hazard-rate parametrizations and attempt to maximize an estimated gap between \(\mathrm{OPT}\) (approximated via LP discretizations or known upper bounds) and stagewise VCG revenue with \(n+c\) buyers, providing a computable fitness signal. The main limitation is that faithfully evaluating \(\mathrm{OPT}\) is computationally expensive and approximation-dependent, so the signal may be noisy or only indicative.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
